text: This video highlights the importance of using a circular anal dilator to both diagnose and guide fixation of internal rectal prolapse during rectal prolapse surgery, getting it right first time.

Internal rectal prolapse is full thickness interception of the rectum. Whilst it can occur in normal individuals, it has been shown to be morphologically more advanced in symptomatic patients with more high-grade or rectoanal interception present.

Patients are usually seen in the pelvic floor clinic and then we'll have specialist investigations. While some centers use conventional x-ray dynamic defecating proctography, we use MRI, which provides higher quality images of the pelvic floor and viscera.
This sequence of MRI images shows a patient with ODS due to a high-grade recto-anal interception. MRI also allows accurate management of all three pelvic floor compartments.
However, whilst providing accurate images, MRI can both understage and also fail to diagnose rectal interception. We have found this looking at the data from our own unit.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a clear circular anal dilator to accurately identify and assess the grade of internal rectal prolapse, utilizing the Oxford grading system. The dilator provides a clear view of the prolapse, allowing for the observation of anterior or posterior predominance.

As well as for diagnosing internal rectal prolapse, crucially, we advocate the use of a CAD during corrective surgery to ensure that the IRP has been fully reduced prior to fixation of the rectum to ensure anatomical correction.

This first video highlights the importance of using the circular anal dilator at the time of surgery to ensure that the rectum is adequately elevated out of the pelvis to reduce the internal rectal prolapse.

The first patient is a sixty-year-old lady with severe ODS but fortunately without concomitant faecal incontinence. Her risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse include three previous vaginal deliveries and also a hysterectomy.

MRDP whilst confirming a full thickness rectal interception showed this only to be intra-arrectal. MRI also identified pelvic floor descent and a rectocele. A transit study showed no evidence of slow transit constipation.
Treatment was initiated with correct positioning during defecation laxatives and pelvic floor physiotherapy. Despite these, her symptoms persisted and the patient requested surgery.

At EUA, an Oxford Grade III IRP was found and due to significant sigmoid pathology, an attempt at VMR was abandoned. She was subsequently rediscussed and offered resection rectopexy. A second EUA had identified progression of the degree of IRP, which was now Grade IV posteriorly.

The surgeon mobilizes the redundant sigmoid loop back to the embryological midline, revealing evidence of previous diverticulitis. The rectum is mobilized down to the pelvic floor in the TME-HODY plane. Following sigmoid resection, a stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused via endoscopy.

Three months following the procedure, the patient reports improved stool frequency and her ODS symptoms have improved. She is very satisfied with the outcome of surgery but longer term data is awaited.

In our second patient, we again demonstrate the value of the CAD to help guide correction of internal rectal prolapse. This patient is a seventy three year old lady with long standing ODS who has started to now also experience faecal incontinence.

She had significant risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse and pelvic floor weakness. A full pelvic floor workup confirmed high-grade rectal interception on MRDP with normal sphincter pressures at manometry.
The endoanal ultrasound scan reveals anatomically intact anal sphincters, with a visibly thickened internal anal sphincter, a common finding in internal rectal prolapse (IRP).

Optimal medical management was instigated, but despite this, at six months follow-up, she was no better clinically. Discussion at the pelvic floor MDT led to a recommendation of laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy to correct the IRP.

During the examination under anesthesia (EUA) at the time of surgery, a grade four internal rectal prolapse is confirmed. Laparoscopy reveals severe diverticular disease with the sigmoid colon adherent to the peritoneum over the vaginal vault.
Surgery was abandoned in favour of rediscussion at the pelvic floor MDT. Following this, the patient was offered reception rectopexy and counselled fully about the risks and benefits of surgery.

The surgeon frees the sigmoid colon from the vaginal vault, revealing a deep and wide pelvis. After dividing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), the rectum is fully mobilized in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane down to the pelvic floor. The sigmoid colon is resected, and a stapled side-to-end anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused on flexible sigmoidoscopy.
When reviewed at twelve months post-surgery, the patient was considerably better with no further incontinence episodes and also resolution of the ADF symptoms, delighted with her outcome.

In conclusion, we believe that the management of all patients with internal rectal prolapse ought to always commence conservatively, using the salt-to-cooking mantra. In cases that fail conservative management, we advocate discussion in a pelvic floor MDT, And in those select cases deemed appropriate for surgery, we strongly recommend the use of a circular anal dilator to ensure complete anatomical reduction of the prolapse. Thank you for listening.
